text.title: Hybrid Approach Examples
text.summary: Three worked examples: fixed-price hybrid contract, solopreneur Kanban, and first-hire integration plan.

example.note: Situation: fixed-price contract, $100K, 4 months, scope partially defined.
example.code:
Phase 1: Discovery (2 weeks) — Agile - User story workshops to surface real requirements - Prototype 3 key screens for client validation - Finalize and baseline scope for contract
Phase 2: Development (10 weeks) — Scrum - 5 two-week sprints - Weekly demos to client (fulfills "engagement" clause) - Backlog refinement mid-sprint
Phase 3: Deployment (2 weeks) — Predictive - Fixed test plan against acceptance criteria - Staged rollout (internal → pilot users → all) - Documentation and handover package
Governance layer (runs throughout): - Monthly steering committee (predictive cadence) - Sprint reviews serve as delivery evidence - Change requests required for scope changes beyond 10%

example.note: Right-sized for single-person work: minimal ceremony, maximum focus.
example.code:
This Week's Focus (WIP: 3 items maximum) 1. [>] Write blog post 2. [>] Update landing page copy 3. [>] Customer discovery call
Waiting (blocked on external) - Design review from contractor - API access credentials from partner
Done This Week - Email newsletter sent - Invoice sent to client A
Weekly Review (Sunday, 30 min) - What shipped? What moved to Waiting? - What's the priority for next week? - Any waiting items to chase?

section.title: Example 3: Solo Founder First Hire Integration

example.note: Structured onboarding plan for founder moving from individual contributor to team lead.
example.code:
Week 1: Orientation - Share vision, values, and "why we exist" - Walk through codebase with commentary - Explain current processes (written, not verbal) - Define initial responsibilities explicitly
Weeks 2-4: Gradual Ownership Transfer - Pair on first 3 tasks - Review their work thoroughly and explain feedback - Give direct, kind feedback on process not person - Discuss what is working and what isn't
Month 2: Independent Ownership - Assign features end-to-end without pairing - Reduce review from every PR to spot checks - Ask them to propose process improvements - Celebrate first solo ship publicly
Key rule: document everything, assume nothing.

rule.statement: For solopreneur use, cap ceremonies at one weekly review unless the team explicitly requests more; agents over-engineer ceremonies by default.
rule.rationale: Ceremony overhead scales poorly for individuals; one weekly review captures the critical "what shipped, what's stuck" signal without adding coordination tax.

text.reference: Sprint planning template for agile project phases
Scores the five-factor decision and outputs the approach recommendation
